doctrinal_renderings.advaita: Sankara reads 'isvarah' (the one whose nature is governance) as Narayana, the pure inner self (sukladantaratman), who stands (tisthati) in the hrdaya-desa of all beings — not as a separate personal agent but as the ground-consciousness that makes governance possible. The beings are like wooden puppets (darukrtapurusadini) mounted on a machine; the word 'iva' (as if) is crucial: the bhramana is appearance, not independent causal action. Maya here means concealment (chadmana) — the Lord's standing does not compromise non-duality; it is the one Reality appearing as diversity through its own obscuring power.
doctrinal_renderings.visistadvaita: Ramanuja reads 'isvara' as Vasudeva, the all-regulator (sarvaniyamanasila), who stands in the hrdaya-desa as the very site where all impulse toward action or withdrawal arises. The yantra is the body-sense-complex that Isvara himself fashioned; beings are mounted on it and driven according to the qualities (sattva-adi) of his own maya. This is not domination from outside but inner superintendence: BG 15.15 'sarvasyaham hrdi sannivistah' and the Antaryami-brahmana confirm the same teaching — the Lord is the indwelling soul of every soul, distinct from them and sustaining them at every moment.
doctrinal_renderings.dvaita: Madhvacarya left no extant bhasyya on this sloka. Reading reconstructed from Dvaita principles: Hari alone is svatantra (self-dependent); jivas are paratantra (other-dependent) by constitution. The yantra-image confirms eternal ontological dependence — the machine does not will its own spinning. Maya here is Visnu's own inconceivable power, not a shared metaphysical fog; beings are moved 'guna-anugunya' according to karma that Hari adjudicates justly. The hrdaya-sthana is Hari's own real presence inside the jiva, never merging with it. [NOTE: Madhva bhasyya absent from payload; this rendering is inferred from Dvaita siddhanta.]
doctrinal_renderings.suddhadvaita: Vallabha reads the verse as the Brahma-sutra siddhanta stated plainly: Vasudeva, sportively (lilaya) creating all beings from highest to grass-blade (a-brahma-stamba), stands in the hrdaya-akasa as antaryamin — yet remains untouched (nirlepa), like space (akasavat-sarvagata). The yantra is the body-sense complex he himself constructed; maya is not illusion but his own creative sakti driving each being according to its own nature. His self-luminosity (svayamprakasa, pradipavat) means the spinning is itself his lila, not a burden he bears — the controller is never entangled in what he controls.
doctrinal_renderings.bhakti: Sridhara Svami, after surveying Sankhya and Svabhava accounts of dependence, declares: 'now I state my own view (svamatamaah).' Isvara as antaryamin stands in every heart and drives all beings to their respective actions the way a puppeteer (sutradharah) moves wooden figures — or, alternatively, the yantra is the body itself on which the jiva-as-atman rides. The Svetasvatara Upanisad ('eko devah sarvabhutesu gudhah') and the Antaryami-brahmana are cited as corroboration. Bhakti to this indwelling Lord is the one movement that aligns the jiva's motion with the mover.
doctrinal_renderings.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana opens by naming the verse's pivot: having stated dependence on svabhava, Krsna now reveals dependence on Isvara. Narayana stands in the antahkarana of every being, made manifest there as a king is manifest in his capital though he pervades the kingdom (Rama in Uttara-Kosala). The address 'Arjuna' signals: only one with a purified inner organ (suddha-antahkarana, 'sukla') can truly grasp this. Beings are 'atyanta-paratantra' — not loosely dependent but utterly so, like carved figures (daru-nirmita-purusadini) on a puppet-rig driven by maya. Madhusudana holds both registers: metaphysically the mover is the non-dual ground; devotionally he is Narayana whom Arjuna can love and surrender to in the very next verse.

so_what_questions: If Isvara already stands in my heart spinning me like a puppet, what is the status of my sense that I am choosing — is that sense itself part of the spinning?
Sankara inserts 'iva' (as if) into the yantra image. What hangs on that single word — does it save non-duality or merely soften a disturbing claim?
Ramanuja and Vallabha both cite BG 15.15 here. Do they read 'sannivistah' (entered/established) the same way, or does the entry mean something structurally different in Visistadvaita vs Suddhadvaita?
The yantra image appears in no prior BG chapter. Why does Krsna deploy a mechanical metaphor at chapter 18, verse 61, after 60 verses of ethical and metaphysical argument?
Sridhara signals a break from Sankhya and Svabhava accounts ('svamatamaah'). What is the doctrinal stakes of that break — what can the Isvara account do that the others cannot?

Madhusudana addresses Arjuna as 'sukla' (pure-hearted) inside his commentary. Is this exegetically justified by the text, or is it Madhusudana inserting an eligibility condition (adhikara) the verse itself does not state?
If the Lord spins all beings 'guna-anugunena' (according to their qualities), is karma still operative, or has the verse quietly replaced karma with Isvara's direct administration?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you find yourself paralyzed by the weight of a decision, notice: the one who is 'paralyzed' is itself a motion in consciousness. The Advaita reading invites a single question before acting — 'who is spinning here?' — not to produce an answer but to let the question dissolve the illusion of an autonomous spinner.
everyday_applications.visistadvaita: In Ramanuja's reading, every impulse to serve well (kainkarya) arises from Vasudeva's own standing in your heart. Practically: before a meeting or a difficult conversation, pause and locate the one who is already present inside you as the source of your best judgment. Then act from there rather than from anxiety.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Madhva's Dvaita presses one clarifying discipline: stop framing your choices as self-originating. The machine metaphor is not humiliating — it is accurate. The practical corollary is that accountability before Hari replaces self-congratulation and self-blame alike. Take your actions seriously as stewardship, not as self-expression.
everyday_applications.suddhadvaita: Vallabha reads the spinning as Krsna's own lila coursing through you. The everyday application is aesthetic rather than ethical: when work feels mechanical or joyless, it is because you have forgotten the player behind the machine. The Pustimarga response is not to work harder but to restore the awareness that this very routine is his sport.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Sridhara's sutradharah (puppeteer) image is the most concrete in the panel. Practically: when a situation feels completely out of your control — a medical diagnosis, a relationship rupture, a professional collapse — the verse does not promise resolution but it does name the one holding the strings. Bhakti is turning toward that one rather than fighting the strings.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusudana's synthesis offers a two-register practice. Philosophically, recognize that what you call 'my will' is already the Lord's movement. Devotionally, love that mover — not as submission to a tyrant but as gratitude to the one whose standing in your heart is what makes your heart a heart at all.

self_assessment: Six schools rendered with real doctrinal divergence anchored in supplied bhasyya prose; Madhva flagged as inferred; Sankara 'iva' pivot, Ramanuja antaryamin-kainkarya, Vallabha lila-nirlepa, Sridhara svamata-break, Madhusudana sukla-address all foregrounded; 7 so-what questions probe the verse's conceptual pressure-points; everyday-applications are school-distinct and non-platitudinous.
